section.title: Judgment against the Nations
section.sectionRationale: Within the seam of the final woes and the ransomed return, chapters 33 to 35, this chapter is the dark counterpart to chapter 35's bright hope. The LORD summons the whole earth to witness a day of vengeance focused on Edom, the paradigm of hostility to God's people, before chapter 35 answers with the ransomed of the LORD returning to Zion with singing.
section.thesis: The LORD summons every nation to witness His day of vengeance, a sweeping judgment concentrated on Edom as the representative enemy of His people, and He alone can declare with certainty that not one word of His judgment will fail, for He has ordered it by His mouth and gathers it by His Spirit.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the whole earth summoned to listen; cosmic imagery of the LORD's wrath from the heavens; the day of vengeance falling on Edom; Edom's desolation becoming a haunt for wild creatures; and the climactic assurance that the scroll of the LORD will not fail in a single detail.

verses.text: Come near, O nations, to listen; pay attention, O peoples. Let the earth hear, and all that fills it, the world and all that springs from it.

units.label: The sword drinks its fill in the heavens

verses.text: The LORD is angry with all the nations and furious with all their armies. He will {{cherem|devote}} them to destruction; He will give them over to slaughter.
Their slain will be left unburied, and the stench of their corpses will rise; the mountains will flow with their blood.
All the stars of heaven will be dissolved. The skies will be rolled up like a scroll, and all their stars will fall like withered leaves from the vine, like foliage from the fig tree.

units.label: The LORD's day of vengeance against Edom

verses.text: When My sword has drunk its fill in the heavens, then it will come down upon Edom, upon the people I have devoted to destruction.

The sword of the LORD is bathed in blood. It drips with fat, with the blood of lambs and goats, with the fat of the kidneys of rams. For the LORD has a sacrifice in Bozrah, a great slaughter in the land of Edom.
And the wild oxen will fall with them, the young bulls with the strong ones. Their land will be drenched with blood, and their soil will be soaked with fat.
For the LORD has a day of {{naqam|vengeance}}, a year of recompense for the cause of Zion.

units.label: Edom becomes a haunt for wild creatures

verses.text: Edom's streams will be turned to tar, and her soil to sulfur; her land will become a blazing pitch.
It will not be quenched, day or night. Its smoke will ascend forever. From generation to generation it will lie desolate; no one will ever again pass through it.
The desert owl and screech owl will possess it, and the great owl and raven will dwell in it. The LORD will stretch out over Edom a measuring line of chaos and a plumb line of destruction.
No nobles will be left to proclaim a king, and all her princes will come to nothing.
Her towers will be overgrown with thorns, her fortresses with thistles and briers. She will become a haunt for jackals, an abode for ostriches.
The desert creatures will meet with hyenas, and one wild goat will call to another. There the night creature will settle and find her place of repose.
There the owl will make her nest; she will lay and hatch her eggs and gather her brood under her shadow. Even there the birds of prey will gather, each with its mate.

units.label: Search the scroll of the LORD

verses.text: Search and read the scroll of the LORD: Not one of these will go missing, not one will lack her mate, because He has ordered it by His mouth, and He will gather them by His Spirit.
He has allotted their portion; His hand has distributed it by measure. They will possess it forever; they will dwell in it from generation to generation.

cherem.gloss: To devote something entirely to destruction, placing it under an irrevocable ban given over wholly to the LORD's judgment. It is the strongest word Hebrew has for total, given-over ruin, used here of the nations under God's righteous anger, and it underlines that this judgment is not random violence but a solemn, deliberate act of divine justice.

naqam.gloss: Vengeance or righteous recompense. This is not personal vindictiveness but the LORD's own settling of accounts for the cause of Zion, repaying cruelty that human courts could never fully address. Scripture reserves final vengeance for God alone, Romans 12:19, and this chapter shows Him exercising it in real history.

basic.context: This chapter is a sweeping courtroom scene: the LORD summons every nation and the whole earth to witness His day of vengeance, concentrated here on Edom, the nation descended from Esau that repeatedly turned against Israel. The imagery escalates from a sword drenched in blood to the stars themselves dissolving, then narrows to a picture of Edom's cities becoming a permanent haunt for owls, jackals, and wild goats.
basic.hermeneutics: Edom functions here as a representative example of hostility to God's people and to God Himself, so the judgment described, while aimed historically at Edom, carries a scope that reaches toward all nations and toward the final Day of the Lord. The cosmic language of the stars dissolving is Isaiah's regular way of describing the collapse of a proud power's whole world order, language the New Testament reuses for the final judgment.
basic.originalLanguages: The nations are {{cherem}}, devoted to destruction, a term for total, irrevocable ruin given over to the LORD. This is the LORD's day of {{naqam}}, vengeance, for the cause of Zion, righteous recompense reserved for God alone rather than human retaliation. The chapter closes by insisting this is written in the LORD's own scroll, gathered by His Spirit, so that not one detail of it will fail.
basic.typology: The collapsing skies and falling stars of verse 4 anticipate the New Testament's language for the final Day of the Lord, when Christ returns to judge the nations in full and final measure. Edom's desolation, guaranteed down to the last detail in the LORD's scroll, foreshadows the certainty of the final judgment recorded in the books opened before Christ's throne.
basic.christ: The cosmic collapse pictured here, stars falling like withered leaves, is language Jesus Himself uses for His own return in glory, Matthew 24:29, and Revelation reuses it for the final judgment, Revelation 6:13-14. All judgment has been given to the Son, Acts 17:31, John 5:22, so this chapter's certain, detailed reckoning against Edom is a preview of the final and complete judgment Christ will execute at His return.
basic.god: God is not a passive observer of history but its righteous judge, summoning the nations to His courtroom and reserving vengeance for Himself rather than leaving it to human hands. His judgments are neither careless nor random; He orders each one by His own mouth and gathers it by His Spirit, so that not a single detail of what He has declared will go missing.
basic.crossReferences: Obadiah 1:1-21 pronounces a similar judgment on Edom for its violence against Jacob. Malachi 1:2-4 recalls this desolation as proof of the LORD's love for Jacob over Esau. Matthew 24:29 and Revelation 6:13-14 reuse this cosmic collapse imagery for the final Day of the Lord. Romans 12:19 reserves vengeance for God alone.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows the LORD as judge of every nation, reserving vengeance for Himself and guaranteeing His judgments down to the last detail. It previews the final Day of the Lord, when Christ, to whom all judgment has been given, will complete exactly this kind of reckoning against every power that has set itself against God and His people.

context.literary: A courtroom summons, verse 1, followed by escalating cosmic and martial imagery, verses 2 to 8, narrowing to a vivid picture of Edom's permanent desolation, verses 9 to 15, and closing with an appeal to the certainty of the LORD's own written scroll, verses 16 to 17.
context.historical: Edom, descended from Esau, repeatedly opposed Israel and Judah across the centuries, including gloating over Jerusalem's fall, Obadiah 1:10-14, Psalm 137:7, making it a fitting representative target for judgment on all nations hostile to God's people.
context.canonical: Part of a wider prophetic tradition of oracles against Edom, alongside Obadiah, Jeremiah 49:7-22, Ezekiel 25:12-14, and Amos 1:11-12, and it functions as the dark counterpart within this seam to chapter 35's bright vision of the ransomed returning to Zion.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare that the LORD is judge over every nation, that Edom's specific hostility to God's people will be repaid in full, and that His judgments, however sweeping in scope, are certain in every detail because they are written in His own scroll.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's righteous judgment on Edom and, through it, on every nation; it is not prescribing human violence or vengeance against enemies, which Scripture reserves to God alone. Its abiding truth is that God is the judge of all the earth and that vengeance belongs to Him, not to His people.

debates.question: Is the collapsing sky of verse 4 literal cosmic destruction or figurative language for Edom's political and social collapse?

views.label: Figurative language for a fallen power's world
views.case: Isaiah regularly uses cosmic collapse, darkened sun, falling stars, as vivid poetic shorthand for the total collapse of a proud nation's world order, as in the oracle against Babylon in chapter 13; the focus in this chapter remains squarely on Edom's land and cities.

views.label: A preview of the final, literal Day of the Lord
views.case: The New Testament reuses this very imagery for Christ's actual return and the final judgment, Matthew 24:29, Revelation 6:13-14, suggesting Isaiah's language, whatever its immediate reference to Edom, genuinely anticipates a final cosmic reckoning.

debates.bearing: Both readings can stand together: a real historical judgment on Edom described in the cosmic language that also, by design, previews the final and complete Day of the Lord.

study.originalLanguages: The nations under judgment are {{cherem}}, devoted to destruction, the strongest term Hebrew has for total, given-over ruin. This is the LORD's day of {{naqam}}, vengeance for the cause of Zion, reserved for God rather than for human hands to seize. The chapter's confidence rests on the LORD's own scroll, ordered by His mouth and gathered by His Spirit, so that not one word of it will fail.

typology.type: The stars dissolving and the skies rolled up like a scroll
typology.fulfillment: The cosmic imagery Jesus and Revelation apply to the final Day of the Lord at Christ's return, Matthew 24:29, Revelation 6:13-14.

typology.type: The certain, detailed scroll of the LORD's judgment on Edom
typology.fulfillment: The certainty of the final judgment recorded before Christ's throne, Revelation 20:11-15.

study.christ: The falling stars and rolled-up skies of this chapter are the very images Jesus uses for His own return in glory, Matthew 24:29, and Revelation applies to the final Day of the Lord, Revelation 6:13-14. Because the Father has given all judgment to the Son, Acts 17:31, John 5:22, this sweeping and certain reckoning against Edom stands as a preview of the complete and final judgment Christ will carry out, from which only those covered by His own atoning sacrifice will be spared.
study.god: God summons the whole earth as witnesses to His justice, showing He is no local or partisan deity but judge of every nation. He reserves vengeance for Himself rather than leaving it to human retaliation, and He guarantees that His judgments, however vast in scope, will not fail in a single detail, because He has both spoken and ordered them by His own Spirit.

crossReferences.note: Pronounces the LORD's judgment on Edom specifically for its violence against Jacob, paralleling this chapter's focus.
Recalls Edom's lasting desolation as evidence of the LORD's covenant love for Jacob over Esau.
Jesus uses the same cosmic collapse imagery, stars falling, for His own return in glory.
Reserves vengeance for God alone, the same principle underlying this chapter's day of vengeance for Zion's cause.

study.oneStory: This chapter reveals the LORD as judge of every nation, reserving vengeance for Himself and guaranteeing His word down to the last detail, using Edom as the representative case of hostility to God and His people. It anticipates the final Day of the Lord, when Christ, to whom all judgment belongs, will complete exactly this kind of certain and total reckoning, sparing only those who take refuge in Him.

quiz.q: Who is summoned as witnesses to the LORD's judgment in verse 1?

options.text: All the nations and the whole earth with everything that fills it.
options.note: Verse 1 opens with this universal summons to listen.

options.text: Only the priests of Jerusalem.
options.note: The summons is universal, not limited to Israel's priesthood.

options.text: Only the armies of Assyria.
options.note: The chapter's focus narrows to Edom, but the opening summons is to every nation, not one army.

options.text: Only the elders of Edom.
options.note: Edom becomes the specific focus later, but the opening summons is universal, to all nations and the earth.

quiz.q: Which nation becomes the specific focus of the LORD's day of vengeance in this chapter?

options.text: Edom, called the people the LORD has devoted to destruction.
options.note: Verses 5 to 8 name Edom and Bozrah specifically as the object of this judgment.

options.text: Babylon.
options.note: Babylon receives its own oracle elsewhere in Isaiah, but this chapter's focus is Edom.

options.text: Egypt.
options.note: Egypt is addressed in other Isaiah oracles, but this chapter specifically names Edom and Bozrah.

options.text: No nation is named; the judgment remains entirely abstract.
options.note: Edom and its city Bozrah are named directly in verses 5 to 6.

quiz.q: What does the description of Edom in verses 11 to 15 depict?

options.text: Its cities becoming a permanent haunt for owls, jackals, hyenas, and wild goats.
options.note: These verses describe exactly this kind of total, lasting desolation overtaken by wild creatures.

options.text: Edom's cities being peacefully resettled by its former citizens.
options.note: The opposite; the land is left desolate and uninhabited by people, given over to wild creatures.

options.text: Edom's temporary abandonment for a single generation only.
options.note: The text specifies desolation from generation to generation, not a brief or single-generation event.

options.text: Edom's transformation into a thriving agricultural region.
options.note: The opposite; the land becomes overgrown with thorns and briers, not cultivated fields.

quiz.q: What claim does verse 16 make about the LORD's scroll of judgment?

options.text: Not one detail of it will go missing, because He has ordered it by His mouth and gathers it by His Spirit.
options.note: Verse 16 makes this exact claim about the total reliability of the LORD's declared judgment.

options.text: The scroll's contents are uncertain and subject to change.
options.note: The opposite; the verse insists on the scroll's total certainty and reliability.

options.text: Only part of the judgment described will actually occur.
options.note: The verse insists that not one of these details will fail or go missing.

options.text: The scroll only concerns events already fulfilled in the past.
options.note: The scroll concerns the judgment being announced in this very chapter, not merely past events.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's cosmic imagery connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Jesus and Revelation reuse the same imagery of falling stars and collapsing skies for the final Day of the Lord.
options.note: Matthew 24:29 and Revelation 6:13-14 apply this very imagery to Christ's return and the final judgment.

options.text: The New Testament rejects this imagery as purely mythological.
options.note: The opposite; the New Testament directly reuses this imagery for a real future event.

options.text: The imagery is applied only to a past event with no future significance.
options.note: The New Testament applies it specifically to a still-future event, Christ's return and final judgment.

options.text: The imagery describes only a minor, localized weather event.
options.note: The scale of the imagery, all the stars, the skies rolled up, points to a cosmic and final scope, not a local event.

questions: The LORD reserves vengeance for Himself rather than leaving it to human hands. Where are you tempted to take justice into your own hands instead of trusting God's timing?
Edom stands here as a representative of hostility to God's people. Where might you be quietly hostile toward those who belong to God, even without realizing it?
The chapter insists that not one word of the LORD's scroll will fail. What promise of God do you most need to trust is certain right now, even though you cannot yet see its fulfillment?
This sweeping judgment anticipates the final Day of the Lord, when Christ will judge all things. How does that certainty shape the way you live today?

Edom's desolation was total and lasting, yet it was matched by Zion's promised restoration in the following chapter. Where in your own life do you need to hold judgment and hope together, trusting God for both?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints an em dash in this verse, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash in this verse, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Bozrah was a chief city of Edom; naming it alongside Edom itself intensifies the specificity and certainty of the judgment described.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 34:1-17. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
